architecture.summary: AgentV-RL is a reward modeling framework that distills a multi-agent verification process into a single LLM. The system coordinates forward and backward agents that perform bidirectional verification: the forward agent traces solutions from premises to conclusions via multi-turn reasoning with tool use (Python interpreter), while the backward agent re-checks conclusions against problem constraints. A synthetic data engine generates verification trajectories, and a two-stage training recipe (rejection fine-tuning followed by GRPO reinforcement learning) trains the verifier to autonomously interleave tool use with internal reasoning for reliable solution assessment.
trained_components: verifier language model (single LLM distilling multi-agent capability)

biblio.title: AgentV-RL: Scaling Reward Modeling with Agentic Verifier

claims.statement: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 79.0% accuracy on MATH500 at Best-of-N (BoN) sampling with N=128, surpassing the previous best outcome-level RM (Skywork-V2-Llama-8B) by 25.2 percentage points
Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B reaches 53.3% accuracy on AIME24 at BoN N=128, while all baselines including larger models remain significantly lower
On GSM8K Turn 1, Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 94.6% accuracy with a correction rate (C_rate) of 41.6% and degradation rate (D_rate) of 0.6% for iterative refinement
Scaling model size from 0.6B to 4B parameters with Agentic Verifier yields improvement of 14.0 points on MATH500 at BoN N=128 (from 62.2% to 79.0%)
Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 70.86% on LiveCodeBench and 66.00% on HotpotQA, demonstrating generalization beyond mathematics to competitive code and multihop QA

Agentic Verifier produces 8349 tokens over 11.3 rounds with 1.6 tool calls per verification trajectory, requiring 323.4 seconds latency on A100 GPU (batch size 128), compared to 2560 tokens and 119.0 seconds for the base model

The bidirectional design (forward + backward agents) achieves state-of-the-art performance; ablation shows both forward-only and backward-only variants are competitive but the full design performs best

The reliance on synthetic, tool-augmented data may not fully represent the variety of real-world reasoning problems, which impede generalization. The multi-turn process increases computational cost, presenting challenges for real-time or resource-limited deployment. Performance is tied to the coverage and reliability of external tools, which can be a bottleneck for certain tasks.
